summary: French Pointillist/Neo-Impressionist painter. Best known for Portrait of Félix Fénéon (full title Opus 217, depicting the goateed art critic holding a top hat, cane, and cyclamen flower before swirling colorful patterns; now at MoMA). Developed pointillism with Georges Seurat, inspired by Charles Blanc's color theory. Wrote From Eugène Delacroix to Neo-Impressionism. Painted In the Time of Harmony, Sunday, and scenes at Saint-Tropez and Marseille. Bought Matisse's Luxe, Calme et Volupté, helping launch Fauvism.

works.name: Portrait of Félix Fénéon (1890)
works.description: Signac's best-known work. Full title: Opus 217: Against the Enamel of a Background Rhythmic with Beats and Angles, Tones, and Tints. Depicts the goateed anarchist art critic Félix Fénéon in profile, holding a top hat, cane, and a white cyclamen flower in front of a swirling, colorful background of green-and-red ripples and yellow stars on blue, demonstrating his Divisionist style. Now housed at the MoMA. Fénéon coined the term 'Neo-Impressionism' and was a defendant in the Trial of the Thirty.

works.name: In the Time of Harmony (1893–1895)
works.description: Large painting subtitled 'the Golden Age is not in the Past, it is in the Future.' Depicts people relaxing by the sea: a man picks a fig from a tree, a woman feeds a fig to her child, two men play boules, a rooster, a hen, dancers under a tree, a painter with an easel, and bowlers. Henri Edmond Cross contrasted its 'dreamed-of age of happiness' with the 'poignant misery' of other anarchist paintings.

examples: Henri Edmond Cross contrasted the 'dreamed-of age of happiness and well-being' depicted in this painting with the 'poignant misery' of other anarchist paintings

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: French Pointillist/Divisionist/Neo-Impressionist. Developed pointillism with Georges Seurat, inspired by Charles Blanc's theories about colors 'mixing' in the eye. Wrote From Eugène Delacroix to Neo-Impressionism. Criticized 'muddy mixtures.' Bought Matisse's Luxe, Calme et Volupté, inspiring Fauvism; later said Matisse had 'gone to the dogs' regarding The Joy of Life. Co-founded the Salon des Indépendants. Frequently depicted Saint-Tropez and Marseille. Said Seurat created 'the most beautiful painter's drawings in existence.' Sailboats appear in several works including Capo di Noli and Women at the Well. Was a juror for the Prix Blumenthal.

comprehensive_summary:
Paul Signac was a French Neo-Impressionist painter who developed pointillism alongside Georges Seurat, inspired by Charles Blanc's theories about colors 'mixing' in the eye. His best-known work is the Portrait of Félix Fénéon (1890), whose full title is Opus 217: Against the Enamel of a Background Rhythmic with Beats and Angles, Tones, and Tints. It depicts the goateed anarchist art critic in profile, holding a top hat, cane, and a white cyclamen flower before a swirling, colorful background of green-and-red ripples and yellow stars on blue. The painting is now housed at the MoMA. Fénéon coined the term 'Neo-Impressionism' and was a defendant in the Trial of the Thirty.

Signac's large painting In the Time of Harmony (subtitled 'the Golden Age is not in the Past, it is in the Future') shows an idyllic scene of people relaxing by the sea, including men playing boules, a man picking a fig from a tree, a woman feeding her child, dancers under a tree, and a painter with an easel. Henri Edmond Cross contrasted its 'dreamed-of age of happiness' with the 'poignant misery' of other anarchist works. Another painting, Sunday, depicts a brown-and-white cat on a Persian rug as a woman in violet stands at the window.

Signac wrote From Eugène Delacroix to Neo-Impressionism, in which he criticized 'muddy mixtures' and echoed a Romantic painter's claim that 'the enemy of all painting is grey.' He purchased Matisse's Luxe, Calme et Volupté, inspiring Matisse toward 'color structure' and the beginning of Fauvism, though he later said Matisse had 'gone to the dogs' regarding The Joy of Life. He said Seurat created 'the most beautiful painter's drawings in existence.' He co-founded the Salon des Indépendants with Seurat and Redon, served as a juror for the Prix Blumenthal, and frequently depicted scenes at Saint-Tropez and Marseille. Sailboats appear in several works including Capo di Noli and Women at the Well. His works were deliberately barred from the 1889 Volpini Exhibition, which instead debuted Gauguin's Post-Impressionist works.
